Mosquito Watch

Dengue, malaria and chikungunya across India, tracked through three independent signals: breeding weather, fever searches and lab testing.
Last updated: Jun 1, 2026
Updated daily 1
Breeding Weather

Is the weather right now good for mosquitoes to breed?

From Open-Meteo
→
Updated weekly 2
Fever Signal

How many people are looking up fever and symptoms online.

From Google Trends
→
Coming soon 3
Fever Panel

How many fever tests people book, and how often they come back positive.

From PharmEasy Diagnostics

The three usually light up in sequence: favourable weather first, then more fever searches, then more testing a week or two later. We keep them separate, never merged into one score.

Layer 1Breeding-favourability map

weather as of 2026-06-01
🦟
What the score means

Each city gets a number from 0 to 100 for how well this week's weather (heat, humidity and rain) suits mosquito breeding. Low means the weather works against them; high means conditions are about as good as they get. It is about the weather only, not medical advice: it doesn't count cases or your own chance of illness, and the air temperature here is not a fever.

Each dot is a city. The bigger and redder it is, the better this week's weather suits mosquito breeding. Click any dot to see the numbers behind it.

Very High 3High 4Moderate 5Low 20
A dot's size and colour both come from its 0 to 100 score. Once again, this is about breeding weather, not reported cases.

City risk table

Sorted by score, 10 cities per page. Click a heading to re-sort, or a row to jump to it on the map.
Score is how good this week's weather is for mosquito breeding, on a scale of 0 to 100. The higher the number, the better the weather is for mosquitoes. Level just sorts that score into easy bands. Both are about the weather, not real cases.
Very High75-100
About as good as it gets for breeding right now.
High50-74
The weather is working in the mosquitoes' favour.
Moderate25-49
Some of the things mosquitoes need are in place.
Low0-24
The weather is mostly working against breeding.

These bands just compare cities against each other. They are not the chance of mosquitoes being present, of breeding being confirmed, of disease spreading, or of anyone falling ill.

9PuneMaharashtra44.0/100Moderate29.263.015.7
10PatnaBihar43.6/100Moderate29.569.61.2

How we work out the score

▸

We show exactly how each score is worked out, so anyone can check it or adjust it. It comes from four things about the weather, combined like this:

Score = 100 × temperature fit × ( 0.30 humidity + 0.25 recent rain + 0.45 earlier rain )

Temperature acts like a gatekeeper. If it's too cold or too hot, mosquitoes won't breed no matter how wet it is, so the score drops close to zero. Once the temperature lands in their comfort zone, the rest comes down to how much standing water and humidity there is.

There's one twist with rain: it helps, but only up to a point. A heavy downpour can wash eggs and larvae out of the puddles they live in, so a real deluge actually pulls the score down rather than pushing it up.

Grounded in published research

This follows a well-established line of Indian research that maps mosquito-borne disease risk by combining environmental layers (water, moisture, vegetation, temperature) with transparent weights. Ours is a lightweight, daily version that uses recent weather in place of satellite imagery. For example, Palo and colleagues (2025) mapped risk zones in Cuttack, Odisha; Ali and Ahmad (2019) used a GIS and weighted-overlay method for Kolkata; and Kumar and Agrawal (2020) mapped vector habitats in Gorakhpur. The temperature band follows Mordecai and colleagues (2017), who found that Aedes-borne transmission runs roughly from 18 to 34°C and peaks around 26 to 29°C.

What each level means

Where the data comes from, and what it can't tell you

Where the weather comes from

Weather data is pulled for each city by its location.

Where it falls short

  • It only looks at the weather. It doesn't count real mosquitoes, infections or cases.
  • It's tuned for the mosquitoes behind dengue and chikungunya (Aedes). Malaria, Japanese encephalitis and filariasis come from other mosquitoes that breed in different places, so treat those as only loosely covered until we add dedicated modules.
  • We take one reading near the middle of each city, so very local spots like building sites or stored water won't show up.
  • Treat the score as a rough guide, not an exact measurement.
  • One more time: the air temperature here is just weather. It is not a fever.

Why we split it into three

Layer 1, the one you're looking at, is all about the weather. Layer 2, further down this page, watches how often people search for fever and symptoms. Layer 3, at the bottom, will bring in PharmEasy's own Fever Panel lab testing: how many fever tests people book and how often they come back positive (a preview on sample data for now, until the live feed is connected). We keep the three apart instead of rolling them into one number, because that is more honest and easier to trust.

Layer 2Fever Signal (search attention)

Sample data. The numbers below are synthetic, generated for development and design. They are not real Google Trends figures. We switch on a real search source before this goes public.
🔎
What this is

How often people across India search for fever and symptom terms, this monsoon season against last year's, so you can see whether attention is running higher or lower than the same week a year ago. It is a sign of attention, not a count of cases: relative on a 0 to 100 scale, and only reliable at state level (so a city shows its state's signal). It stands on its own, not added to the weather score above.

Pick a signal:

Searches across India

Last year's full season (grey, dashed) against this year so far (solid), normalised together so they line up week for week. Read this year against the same week last year, not last year's later peak. Pick a city to see its state (cities in a state share one trend; Google Trends does not resolve these by city).

Search interest is relative and normalised, not a tally of people or of cases. A single news story can lift searches everywhere at once. Read it next to official surveillance, never as a diagnosis.

Layer 3Fever Panel (lab testing)

Preview, sample data
Coming soon, sample data. These Fever Panel numbers are synthetic placeholders for design. The live version will draw on PharmEasy's own lab diagnostics, refreshed weekly. Not real test data yet.
🧪
What this is

PharmEasy's Fever Panel is a lab test for people running a fever. This layer shows two things, week by week and this monsoon season against last: how many panels people book (a sign of demand) and how often they come back positive for each disease (a sign of real activity). It is a lab-testing signal, not a count of every case, and it stands on its own. It is not added to the weather score or the search signal above.

Show:
Disease:

Fever Panel tests booked across India

Last year's full season (grey, dashed) against this year so far (solid). Both lines share one scale. Read this year against the same week last year, not last year's later peak.

A year-over-year chart of PharmEasy Fever Panel tests booked across India through the monsoon season. Sample data; full series in data/panel_signal.json.

Sample data, shown for design. When live, these come from PharmEasy's Fever Panel lab diagnostics: a testing signal, not a full case count and not a diagnosis. Read it next to official surveillance and your doctor's advice.

Questions people ask

Is dengue, malaria or chikungunya risk high in my city right now?

Mosquito Watch shows a breeding-favourability score from 0 to 100 for major Indian cities, based only on this week's weather (temperature, humidity and recent rain). A high score means the weather suits mosquito breeding. It is a screening and comparison guide, not a count of cases and not a measure of your personal chance of illness.

Where does the data come from?

Three independent sources, kept separate. Breeding weather comes from Open-Meteo. The fever signal comes from Google Trends search interest. The third signal, a Fever Panel testing view, is coming soon and will draw on PharmEasy's own lab diagnostics: how many fever tests people book and how often they come back positive.

Is this medical advice or a case forecast?

No. Mosquito Watch is an environmental risk and surveillance dashboard, not a diagnostic or predictive tool. It never predicts individual illness or forecasts case counts. For diagnosis or treatment consult a doctor, and for official alerts follow your state health department and local public-health sources.

How often is it updated?

Breeding weather updates daily and the fever signal weekly. The Fever Panel testing signal is a preview on sample data for now; when it goes live it will refresh weekly from PharmEasy's lab diagnostics.

What do the risk levels mean?

Scores are grouped into four bands: Low (0 to 24), Moderate (25 to 49), High (50 to 74) and Very High (75 to 100). The bands compare cities against each other by weather only. They are not the chance of mosquitoes being present, of disease spreading, or of anyone falling ill.

Why keep the three layers separate instead of a single score?

Because they measure different things on different timelines. Weather tends to lead, fever searches follow, and testing comes last. Keeping them apart is more honest and easier to check than blending them into one number.

This is a weather-based guide, nothing more. Please don't treat it as official health guidance or medical advice.
